rooms.bio: Dutch post-impressionist (1853-1890) who turned paint itself into feeling - thick, directional strokes that vibrate with colour. He sold almost nothing in his lifetime and changed everything after it.

pieces.title: The Bedroom
pieces.story: His own room at Arles, painted to feel like rest itself. The tilted perspective and flat, saturated colour were radical: a room painted the way it felt, not the way it looked.

pieces.title: Self-Portrait
pieces.story: Painted in Paris while absorbing the pointillists. Too poor to hire models, Van Gogh turned the mirror on himself dozens of times - each self-portrait an experiment in colour theory.

rooms.bio: Founder of French impressionism (1840-1926), obsessed with light as the true subject of painting. He painted the same motifs over and over as the hours changed them.

pieces.title: Water Lilies
pieces.story: From the pond he built at Giverny and painted for thirty years. There is no horizon - only water, reflection and bloom, dissolving painting into pure surface.

pieces.title: Stacks of Wheat (End of Summer)
pieces.story: One canvas from the series that made seriality itself the subject: the same haystacks, dawn to dusk, summer to frost, until time became visible.

pieces.title: Cliff Walk at Pourville
pieces.story: Wind, grass and sea air on the Normandy coast. Look at the two figures: barely a few strokes each, yet unmistakably leaning into the breeze.

pieces.title: Arrival of the Normandy Train
pieces.story: Impressionism walks into the machine age: steam, iron and glass treated with the same reverence as sunsets. Monet set up his easel inside the working station.

rooms.bio: Inventor of pointillism (1859-1891), who rebuilt painting from optical science - millions of pure-colour dots that fuse in the viewer’s eye.

pieces.title: A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
pieces.story: Two years and millions of dots to freeze one Parisian Sunday into geometry. Seurat painted with discrete points of colour - a picture computed by the eye, a century before pixels.

rooms.bio: Painter and patron of the impressionists (1848-1894), whose sharp urban realism captured the new Paris of boulevards, iron and glass.

pieces.title: Paris Street; Rainy Day
pieces.story: The radical geometry of a modern city in the rain: a lamppost splits the canvas, umbrellas repeat into the distance, and strangers pass without meeting eyes.

rooms.bio: Japanese ukiyo-e master (1760-1849) whose woodblock prints crossed the ocean and rewired European art. He signed late work "the old man mad about painting".

pieces.title: The Great Wave off Kanagawa
pieces.story: The most reproduced image in art history: a wave like a claw, boats like slivers, and tiny, immovable Fuji at the centre. Printed from carved woodblocks, thousands of times.

rooms.bio: French impressionist (1841-1919) devoted to warmth: skin, sunlight, and the pleasure of ordinary afternoons.

pieces.title: Two Sisters (On the Terrace)
pieces.story: A riverside terrace at Chatou. The little girl’s hat is almost pure pigment - Renoir letting paint be paint exactly where the eye wants sweetness.

rooms.bio: The bridge between impressionism and everything after (1839-1906). Both Matisse and Picasso called him "the father of us all".

pieces.title: The Basket of Apples
pieces.story: A still life that quietly breaks the rules of space: the table edges don’t line up, the bottle leans, each object is seen from its own angle. Cubism starts here.

rooms.bio: American painter in Paris (1844-1926), the only American the impressionists ever invited in. She made domestic life monumental.

pieces.title: The Child’s Bath
pieces.story: Seen from above like a Japanese print: patterns colliding, and at the centre the quiet weight of care - a hand steadying a child’s foot.

rooms.bio: Chronicler of Montmartre nightlife (1864-1901), painting dancers, singers and outsiders with affection and zero sentimentality.

pieces.title: At the Moulin Rouge
pieces.story: Gaslight green and theatre-box framing: a face glows acid at the edge, friends huddle at a table, and the artist paints himself passing in the background.

rooms.bio: Impressionist of movement and artificial light (1834-1917) - ballet rehearsals, racehorses, laundresses - always from unexpected angles.

pieces.title: The Millinery Shop
pieces.story: A hat maker absorbed in her work, crowded to the edge by her own creations. Degas cropped like a photographer decades before it was normal.
